sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("12-pixel objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 12:
        cr = sum(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(b[1] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  ({cr},{cc}) color={n['color']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
(23,37) was NOT removed. The white border just moved to (35,45). So my hypothesis was wrong - clicking on a bordered object doesn't remove the previous white-bordered object.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the earlier sequence:
- Click (42,40): white moved to (42,40), (48,54) removed
- Click (23,41): white moved to (23,41), (9,49) removed
What was special about (42,40) and (23,41)?
- (42,40) was light gray (w), not dark gray (G)
- (23,41) was the orange object (20 pixels), not a 12-pixel bordered object
So maybe the rule is:
- Clicking on a special object (like the orange junction or the light gray bordered object) removes the current white-bordered object
Let me check if there's still a special object. The orange object is at (28,37). Let me click on it to see if it removes (35,45).

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on orange object at (28,37) to remove (35,45)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 37}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
